reasoning: The 30-day SOFR average calculation replaces daily values from 30 days prior. On March 30, the average will include the mid-March dip where daily SOFR fell to 4.29-4.32, with an expected 30-day average of ~4.336. For the April 6 and April 29 resolution dates, the calculation will progressively drop these low mid-March daily rates and replace them with the typically elevated Mar 31 quarter-end spike and standard April rates (~4.34), leading to a near-certain mathematical increase compared to March 30. Thus, the near-term probabilities are high. For June 28 and beyond, the primary driver will be the Fed's rate policy. The CME FedWatch tool currently prices a 62% cumulative probability of a minimum 25-basis-point cut by the June meeting, which would overwhelm any daily fluctuations and drag the 30-day average firmly below 4.336. As time horizons stretch to September 2025 and March 2026, the market anticipates further cuts. However, reflecting the instruction that 'uncertainty should increase with forecast horizon,' the farther dates' probabilities are incrementally driven back towards the 0.50 anchor, representing the long-tail possibilities of reignited inflation or structural liquidity shortages driving up SOFR independently of standard expectations.
